title: The Mongol Empire
summary: The largest contiguous land empire in history — a Eurasian empire founded by Genghis Khan in 1206 that within seventy years stretched from the Pacific to the Danube, destroyed some of the most sophisticated cities in the world, enabled unprecedented trade and exchange across the continent, and carried the plague that became the Black Death.
hook: The Mongol Empire killed more people and moved more goods across greater distances than any political entity before the modern age. In the span of a single lifetime, Genghis Khan's horsemen destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate in Baghdad, devastated the cities of Central Asia, invaded Europe, and reached the coasts of Japan and Vietnam — while simultaneously creating the most open trade corridor in Eurasian history. Understanding the Mongols means holding both facts simultaneously: the empire of the greatest violence and the empire of the greatest connectivity were the same empire.

beginner.thePeriod: The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous land empire in history. At its peak in the 13th century, it stretched from the Pacific coast of China to the Danube River in Europe. It was founded by Temüjin — a Mongol chieftain who unified the nomadic tribes of the steppe and took the title Genghis Khan in 1206. The Mongols conquered more territory in 25 years than the Romans conquered in 400. They destroyed some of the most sophisticated cities in the world. They also created the conditions for unprecedented trade across Eurasia.
beginner.theBoundaries: The empire began with Genghis Khan's unification of the Mongol tribes in 1206. It expanded rapidly under Genghis and his successors. China fell to Kublai Khan by 1279. The empire formally fragmented into four successor states — the Yuan in China, the Il-Khanate in Persia, the Chagatai Khanate in Central Asia, and the Golden Horde in Russia — in the 1260s. These states continued to rule vast territories but were often at war with each other. The last major Mongol state collapsed in the late 15th century. In China, Mongol rule ended with the Ming Dynasty's founding in 1368.
beginner.theConditions: The Mongols built their empire from the steppe — the great belt of grassland stretching from Hungary to Manchuria. Steppe nomads had specific military advantages. They were superb horsemen from childhood. Their armies were highly mobile. They could sustain campaigns across vast distances. But they were also skilled at absorbing the technologies of peoples they conquered. They used Chinese engineers to attack walled cities. They used Persian administrators to govern conquered territories. The steppe provided the military base. The conquered peoples provided administrative and technical capacity.
beginner.internalDiversity: The Mongol Empire was not uniform. Different khans took it in different directions. In Persia, the Il-Khanate eventually converted to Islam. In China, the Yuan Dynasty adapted to Chinese traditions. In Russia, the Golden Horde collected tribute but left Russian political structures largely intact. The four successor states were often at war with each other. The empire's diversity reflected the scale of its conquest — it ruled too many different peoples across too vast a territory to maintain a single character.
beginner.longConsequences: The Mongol Empire created the Pax Mongolica — a period of relative peace across Eurasia that enabled unprecedented trade and cultural exchange. The Silk Road reached its peak volume. But plague also traveled these routes. The Black Death that killed a third of Europe in the 1340s and 1350s moved along Mongol trade networks from Central Asia. The Mongols destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate in Baghdad in 1258 — ending a great center of Islamic learning. They also transmitted Chinese technologies westward, including papermaking and printing techniques.
beginner.periodizationDebate: Historians debate whether the Mongol Empire should be remembered primarily as destroyer or creator. The destruction was real and documented. Baghdad, Samarkand, Kiev, and other cities were devastated. Millions died. But the period of Mongol peace also enabled extraordinary cross-cultural exchange — goods, ideas, and people moved across Eurasia at a scale that had not existed before. Recent scholarship increasingly emphasizes both dimensions simultaneously rather than choosing between them.

general.thePeriod: The Mongol Empire represents the most dramatic case of steppe nomadic power in world history — a challenge to the assumption that civilizational sophistication and military dominance necessarily go together. The Mongols had no cities, no writing system of their own at the empire's founding, and no agricultural base. Yet they conquered the most urbanized, literate, and agriculturally sophisticated civilizations of the Eurasian continent. The explanation lies partly in military technology (horse archery, mobility, discipline), partly in organizational innovation (the decimal military system, promotion on merit), and partly in the specific political fragmentation of the civilizations they attacked.
general.theBoundaries: The periodization of the Mongol Empire involves distinguishing between the unified empire under the great khans (1206–1260) and the successor states that claimed its inheritance. The unified empire was, in practice, more a network of family-controlled territories than a single state — the khans of the successor states were all members of the Chinggisid dynasty and acknowledged a theoretical hierarchy, but this hierarchy broke down in the succession crisis after Möngke Khan's death in 1259. The Battle of Ain Jalut in 1260 — where the Mamluk sultanate of Egypt defeated a Mongol army — marked the first significant limit on Mongol expansion and is sometimes treated as the effective end of unified Mongol power.
general.theConditions: Thomas Allsen's work on Mongol court culture has shown that the empire was not simply destructive — it actively promoted artisanal production, long-distance trade, and the movement of specialists (craftsmen, administrators, physicians, astronomers) across its territory. The Mongols' willingness to employ talent regardless of ethnic or religious background created a genuinely cosmopolitan administrative culture. Persian became the language of Mongol administration across much of the empire; Chinese administrative traditions dominated in East Asia. The Secret History of the Mongols — the earliest surviving narrative in the Mongolian language — provides an insider perspective on the empire's founding and culture that is unavailable for most ancient empires.
general.internalDiversity: The religious diversity of the Mongol Empire is striking. Genghis Khan himself maintained an eclectic religious openness — he exempted religious leaders of all faiths from taxation. His successors converted to different religions: the Il-Khans of Persia eventually became Muslim; the Yuan emperors of China patronized Tibetan Buddhism; some Golden Horde khans converted to Islam. This diversity reflected the Mongols' characteristic pragmatism — religion was, for them, primarily a political resource rather than a commitment that required exclusivity. The Il-Khanate's conversion to Islam in 1295 was particularly consequential, transforming a potentially hostile relationship with the region's majority population into a basis for legitimacy.
general.longConsequences: The Black Death's relationship to the Mongol Empire is one of the most consequential examples of how political connectivity can accelerate epidemiological spread. Yersinia pestis — the plague bacterium — appears to have been endemic in Central Asian rodent populations for millennia. The Mongol trade networks created the conditions for it to spread rapidly across Eurasia. The siege of Caffa (1346) — where Mongol forces catapulted plague-infected bodies over the walls of a Genoese trading post — may have introduced plague to Europe; the subsequent epidemic killed between 30 and 60 percent of Europe's population. The demographic, economic, and cultural consequences of the Black Death — including the labor shortages that contributed to the end of European serfdom — are thus partly traceable to the Mongol Empire.
general.periodizationDebate: Jack Weatherford's Genghis Khan and the Making of the Modern World argued for a radically positive reassessment of the Mongol Empire — emphasizing religious toleration, meritocracy, international trade, and the transmission of technologies across Eurasia. This revisionism has been influential with general readers but has been criticized by specialist historians who argue it understates the scale and gratuitousness of Mongol violence. David Morgan's more measured assessment treats the Mongols as neither uniquely destructive nor uniquely beneficial but as a political force that interacted with existing civilizations in complex ways that varied enormously by region and period.

advanced.thePeriod: The Mongol Empire poses fundamental methodological challenges to historians. The primary sources are written by the conquered — Persian, Chinese, Arabic, Armenian, and Latin chroniclers — whose perspectives are shaped by the experience of invasion and subjugation. The Mongol perspective is available primarily through the Secret History of the Mongols, a text whose original composition and purpose remain disputed, and through the administrative records of the successor states. Thomas Allsen's work on Mongol court culture has used these sources creatively to reconstruct a more complex picture than the sources' surface readings suggest, but the fundamental asymmetry of the documentary record shapes what can and cannot be known about Mongol intentions and self-understanding.
advanced.theBoundaries: The question of whether the Mongol Empire should be understood as a single entity or as a family of related but distinct polities has significant implications for how its history is written. Timothy May's The Mongol Empire: A Historical Encyclopedia treats it as a coherent entity with identifiable institutions, strategies, and cultures. Other historians — particularly those specializing in specific successor states — emphasize the discontinuities between the unified empire and its successors, arguing that the Il-Khanate, Yuan Dynasty, and Golden Horde are better understood as distinct Persian, Chinese, and Russian political formations with Mongol ruling dynasties than as expressions of a unified 'Mongol' political culture.
advanced.theConditions: The relationship between Mongol military effectiveness and Mongol political organization has been analyzed most carefully by Timothy May, whose work on the Mongol art of war shows that Mongol military success was not simply a product of nomadic horse-archery (which other steppe peoples also practiced) but of specific organizational innovations: the decimal system of military organization (units of 10, 100, 1000, and 10,000); the use of intelligence networks before campaigns; the systematic exploitation of prisoner expertise; and the systematic use of psychological warfare (cities that resisted were destroyed; cities that surrendered were spared). This organizational sophistication made the Mongol military qualitatively different from earlier steppe raiders.
advanced.internalDiversity: The historiography of the Mongol successor states has been substantially transformed by area specialists who have insisted on reading them in their local contexts rather than primarily as expressions of a pan-Mongol political culture. Reuven Amitai's work on the Mamluk-Mongol conflict emphasizes the specifically Islamic dimensions of Mamluk resistance. John Masson Smith's work on the Golden Horde emphasizes its integration into the political culture of the Eurasian steppe. These specialist accounts complicate the unified narrative of 'Mongol history' while enriching understanding of how Mongol rule was experienced and transformed by the peoples who lived under it.
advanced.longConsequences: The Mongol destruction of Baghdad in 1258 — the sack of the Abbasid Caliphate's capital, which killed the Caliph and perhaps 100,000-800,000 inhabitants — has been treated in Islamic historical memory as a civilizational catastrophe and in Western historiography as a turning point in Islamic intellectual history. The destruction of the House of Wisdom and its libraries represents an unrecoverable loss of texts. The political consequences — the end of the unified Abbasid Caliphate — permanently fragmented Sunni political authority. The later conversion of the Il-Khans to Islam partially repaired the relationship between Mongol rule and Islamic civilization, but the memory of 1258 has retained its power in Islamic political and historical consciousness to the present.
advanced.periodizationDebate: The most productive current debates in Mongol historiography concern the mechanisms and extent of the Mongol transmission of knowledge and technology across Eurasia. Thomas Allsen's Culture and Conquest in Mongol Eurasia documents the systematic movement of artisans, scientists, and administrators across the empire's territory and argues that this movement was not incidental but a deliberate Mongol policy. The question of how much this transmission actually influenced the subsequent development of European science, technology, and commerce — the Pax Mongolica as a precondition for the later European expansion — remains contested but has attracted significant scholarly attention.

foundation.theStory: The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching from the Pacific to the Danube within a single lifetime. It was built through extraordinary military effectiveness by steppe nomads who had no cities, no writing, and no agricultural base — and who then created the most extensive trade network in pre-modern Eurasian history while simultaneously devastating the civilizations they conquered.
foundation.theDebate: The central historical debate concerns how to evaluate the Mongol Empire simultaneously as the greatest act of destruction in medieval history and the greatest enabler of Eurasian connectivity. Was it primarily a catastrophe that destroyed civilizations, or primarily a transformative force that created new connections? The answer depends partly on who is being asked — the descendants of the destroyed cities of Central Asia have different memories than the descendants of societies that benefited from Silk Road trade.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Mongol Empire matters for understanding how steppe societies interacted with agrarian civilizations across Eurasian history, how the Black Death spread (along Mongol trade routes), how Islamic civilization was transformed by the destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate, and how the Silk Road reached its peak. It also matters as a case study in how military effectiveness without cultural sophistication can temporarily overwhelm civilizational complexity.
questions: The Mongols had no cities, no writing system at the empire's founding, and no agricultural base — yet they conquered the most sophisticated civilizations of Eurasia. What specific military, organizational, and political advantages explain this? And what does it tell us about the relationship between cultural sophistication and military power?

The Mongol Empire simultaneously destroyed some of the most sophisticated cities in the world and created the most extensive trade network in Eurasian history. How should historians weigh these contradictory consequences? Does the same entity deserve credit for both, or should the destruction and the connectivity be analyzed separately?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The historiography of the Mongol Empire has moved through several phases. The 19th-century tradition emphasized the destructive dimensions — particularly the impact on Islamic civilization. Jack Weatherford's Genghis Khan and the Making of the Modern World (2004) offered a revisionist popular account emphasizing positive contributions: religious tolerance, meritocracy, trade, and the transmission of technology. This revisionism has been influential with general readers but criticized by specialists. Thomas Allsen's scholarly work on Mongol court culture offers the most sophisticated account of how the empire actually functioned as a political and cultural system.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidentiary challenges for Mongol history are substantial. Most primary sources were written by the conquered, whose perspectives are shaped by the experience of invasion. The Mongol perspective is available primarily through the Secret History of the Mongols and the administrative records of the successor states. Archaeological evidence is limited because the Mongols, as nomads, built few permanent structures. Death-toll estimates for Mongol conquests vary enormously — ranging from 10 to 40 million for the entire period — and reflect both the genuine difficulty of estimation and the political stakes of the question.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible position on the Mongol Empire must avoid two symmetrical errors: romanticizing the violence as the necessary precondition for connectivity (the ends-justify-means argument), and treating the destruction as so overwhelming that the genuine achievements of the Pax Mongolica disappear. The most analytically productive approach treats both as real: the violence was enormous and should not be minimized; the cross-cultural transmission was also real and consequential. The task is to explain how the same political entity produced both outcomes simultaneously.
questions: The Black Death — which killed between 30 and 60 percent of Europe's population — traveled along Mongol trade routes from Central Asia. How should this epidemiological consequence be incorporated into the historical assessment of the Mongol Empire? Does the Mongol facilitation of plague spread change how we evaluate the 'benefits' of Pax Mongolica trade?

The Mongol Empire's Il-Khanate successor state in Persia eventually converted to Islam — the religion of the civilization the Mongols had devastated. What does this conversion tell us about the relationship between conqueror and conquered over time? Is this a case of the conquered culture absorbing the conqueror, or something more complex?

research.topic: Mongol military organization and its role in conquest
research.content: Timothy May's work on the Mongol art of war has established the basic features of Mongol military effectiveness: the decimal organization of forces, the systematic use of intelligence, the psychological warfare strategy (surrender and be spared; resist and be destroyed), and the capacity to conduct coordinated campaigns across vast distances. These organizational features distinguished the Mongols from earlier steppe raiders and explain much of their military success against settled civilizations with superior technology and numbers.

research.topic: The Mongol Empire's role in cross-cultural transmission
research.content: Thomas Allsen's Culture and Conquest in Mongol Eurasia has generated a growing literature on the mechanisms and extent of Mongol facilitation of knowledge and technology transfer across Eurasia. The movement of artisans, physicians, astronomers, and administrators across the empire was systematic and deliberate. Whether this transmission significantly influenced the subsequent development of European science and technology — making the Pax Mongolica a precondition for the later European expansion — remains contested but increasingly studied.

research.topic: Accurate death tolls and the full human cost of conquest
research.content: Estimates of deaths caused by Mongol conquests range from 10 to 40 million people across the empire's entire history. These estimates are essentially unverifiable. What evidence would resolve the question: systematic pre- and post-conquest population surveys, which did not exist in medieval Central Asia, China, or the Middle East. Why unavailable: medieval states lacked the administrative capacity for accurate census-taking; the Mongol conquests themselves destroyed whatever administrative records existed. What can be responsibly inferred: that the demographic impact was massive, that certain regions (Central Asia, Persia, northern China) suffered catastrophic population decline, and that recovery took generations. What must remain unknowable: precise figures, regional variations with confidence, and the relative contribution of direct killing versus famine and disease induced by the destruction of agricultural infrastructure.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Catastrophe of 1258
comparativeNarrative.content: In Islamic historical memory, the Mongol sack of Baghdad in 1258 — the destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate, the killing of the Caliph, and the burning of the House of Wisdom — is one of the defining catastrophes of Islamic civilization. The memory of 1258 has retained extraordinary power across eight centuries. Contemporary political rhetoric in the Middle East still invokes the Mongol destruction as a template for Western intervention. The Il-Khanate's eventual conversion to Islam partially rehabilitated Mongol rule in Islamic historiography, but the memory of Baghdad has never been fully reconciled with a positive assessment of Mongol civilization.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Yuan Dynasty and Its Ambivalences
comparativeNarrative.content: Chinese historical memory of the Mongol period is complex. The Yuan Dynasty — Kublai Khan's Chinese empire — is included in the standard Chinese dynastic sequence, which implicitly incorporates it within Chinese history. But the Mongols are also remembered as foreign conquerors who imposed alien rule. The Ming Dynasty that replaced them in 1368 presented itself explicitly as the restoration of Chinese civilization after Mongol occupation. Contemporary Chinese nationalism navigates this ambivalence carefully: the Mongol period is part of Chinese history, but the Mongols are not Chinese.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Founding Myth
comparativeNarrative.content: The Mongol perspective on the empire — available primarily through the Secret History of the Mongols — presents Genghis Khan's rise as a story of personal destiny, tribal unification, and divine mandate. The Secret History is remarkable both as literature and as historical source: it presents a world of personal loyalty, clan politics, and shamanic cosmology that is quite different from the administrative perspective of Persian and Chinese chronicles. Contemporary Mongolia treats Genghis Khan as a national hero — his face appears on currency and vodka bottles — in a rehabilitation that deliberately inverts the negative assessment of Western and Chinese historical traditions.

rabbitHole.label: The Silk Road
rabbitHole.reason: The Silk Road reached its peak during the Pax Mongolica — the Mongol Empire was the political framework that made trans-Eurasian trade possible at unprecedented scale

rabbitHole.label: The Black Death
rabbitHole.reason: The plague that killed a third of Europe traveled along Mongol trade networks from Central Asia — the Pax Mongolica that enabled trade also enabled the most devastating pandemic in European history

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: The Mongol destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate in Baghdad in 1258 ended a major center of Islamic learning and permanently fragmented Sunni political authority

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: The Mongol Empire raises questions that colonialism scholarship also addresses: how do conquering powers govern culturally different populations, and how do conquered peoples adapt, resist, and eventually absorb their conquerors?

rabbitHole.label: Horses
rabbitHole.reason: The Mongol Empire was fundamentally a horse empire — its military effectiveness, its mobility, and its ability to project power across Eurasia all rested on the horse as a military and logistical technology

rabbitHole.label: Genghis Khan
rabbitHole.reason: A dedicated entry on the founder of the empire — his unification of the Mongol tribes, his military innovations, and his place in global memory — would complement this period entry

rabbitHole.label: The Il-Khanate
rabbitHole.reason: The Mongol successor state in Persia, which converted to Islam and became one of the most culturally productive Mongol states — its patronage of Persian art, architecture, and scholarship represents a complex relationship between conqueror and conquered culture

rabbitHole.label: Genghis Khan and the Making of the Modern World — Jack Weatherford (2004)
rabbitHole.reason: Weatherford's revisionist popular account of the Mongol Empire is the most widely read modern treatment — accessible, provocative, and a useful starting point even where specialists disagree with its conclusions

reference.author: Timothy May
reference.title: The Mongol Empire: A Historical Encyclopedia
reference.annotation: May is the leading Anglophone specialist on Mongol military history and the empire's political organization. This encyclopedia provides the most comprehensive scholarly overview available, and his earlier The Mongol Art of War is the essential account of how Mongol armies actually functioned.

reference.author: Ata-Malik Juvaini
reference.title: Genghis Khan: The History of the World Conqueror
reference.annotation: Juvaini's chronicle, written in Persian by a Mongol-era administrator who witnessed the empire firsthand, is the most important contemporary source on the Mongol conquests of Central Asia and Persia. It combines detailed historical narrative with literary sophistication and is available in J.A. Boyle's English translation.

reference.author: Rashid al-Din
reference.title: Compendium of Chronicles (Jami' al-Tawarikh)
reference.annotation: Rashid al-Din's universal history, commissioned by the Il-Khanate and written in Persian, is the most comprehensive Mongol-era source on the empire's history. It draws on sources in multiple languages and provides detailed accounts of the Mongol dynasties across Eurasia.

reference.author: Thomas Allsen
reference.title: Culture and Conquest in Mongol Eurasia
reference.annotation: Allsen's definitive scholarly account of the Mongol Empire's role in facilitating cross-cultural exchange — the systematic movement of artisans, scientists, and administrators across the empire's territory. Essential for moving beyond the destruction narrative.

reference.author: Jack Weatherford
reference.title: Genghis Khan and the Making of the Modern World
reference.annotation: Weatherford's revisionist popular account emphasizes the Mongol Empire's positive contributions: religious tolerance, meritocracy, trade, and technological transmission. Influential with general readers; criticized by specialists for understating the violence. Worth reading critically alongside May and Allsen.

reference.author: David Morgan
reference.title: The Mongols
reference.annotation: Morgan's measured scholarly overview remains the most balanced single-volume account — less revisionist than Weatherford, more readable than specialized monographs. The essential starting point for the serious general reader.

commerce.title: Genghis Khan and the Making of the Modern World
commerce.author: Jack Weatherford
commerce.note: The most widely read modern account — accessible, revisionist, and a good starting point even where specialists push back

commerce.title: The Mongols
commerce.author: David Morgan
commerce.note: The most balanced scholarly overview — measured, readable, and trustworthy where Weatherford overreaches

commerce.title: The Silk Roads: A New History
commerce.author: Peter Frankopan
commerce.note: Frankopan places the Mongol Empire in the broader context of Eurasian exchange — essential for understanding what the Pax Mongolica made possible

commerce.title: Mongol: The Rise of Genghis Khan — Sergei Bodrov (2007)
commerce.note: The most serious cinematic treatment of Genghis Khan's rise — filmed in Mongolian and Russian, it presents the founding of the empire from inside Mongol culture rather than from the perspective of the conquered

commerce.title: The Secret History of the Mongols — trans. Igor de Rachewiltz
commerce.author: Anonymous (trans. Igor de Rachewiltz)
commerce.note: The earliest surviving Mongolian narrative text and the primary source for Genghis Khan's life — demanding but irreplaceable for understanding the empire from the inside

popularCulture.title: Mongol: The Rise of Genghis Khan — Sergei Bodrov (2007)
popularCulture.description: Bodrov's film depicts Genghis Khan's early life — his capture, enslavement, and rise to power — with epic scope and unusual attention to Mongol cultural context. Filmed in Mongolian and Russian with Mongolian actors, it presents the founding of the empire from inside rather than from the perspective of the conquered civilizations. It is the most serious and historically attentive cinematic treatment of Genghis Khan available.

popularCulture.title: The Conqueror series — Conn Iggulden (2007–2010)
popularCulture.description: Iggulden's five-novel series follows Genghis Khan and his successors across the Mongol conquests. As historical fiction it takes liberties with the record, but it conveys the scale and pace of Mongol expansion — and the experience of living within the empire — with a vividness that pure historical narrative struggles to match. The series is widely read and provides an accessible narrative entry into a period that can feel remote.

popularCulture.title: Marco Polo — Netflix (2014–2016)
popularCulture.description: Netflix's dramatization of Marco Polo's time at the court of Kublai Khan depicts the Yuan Dynasty's cosmopolitan court culture — the religious diversity, the political complexity, and the extraordinary wealth of the Mongol Chinese empire at its height. Though substantially dramatized, it captures the multicultural character of Mongol rule better than most Western popular treatments, which tend to focus solely on the violence of conquest.

popularCulture.title: The Mongol legacy in modern Mongolia
popularCulture.description: Contemporary Mongolia treats Genghis Khan as the supreme national hero — his image appears on currency, airport terminals, and a forty-meter equestrian statue outside Ulaanbaatar. This contemporary Mongolian nationalism inverts the historical memory of most of the world, which remembers the Mongols primarily through the devastation of conquest. The contrast between the Mongol Empire as catastrophe (the memory of Baghdad, Kiev, Samarkand) and the Mongol Empire as foundation (the Mongolian national identity) is one of the most striking examples of how the same history generates incompatible memories.

popularCulture.title: The Mongol Empire as the largest contiguous land empire in history
popularCulture.description: The claim that the Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous land empire in history appears regularly in popular history and educational materials. What it reveals: the scale of the Mongol conquests is genuinely extraordinary — at its height the empire stretched from Korea to Hungary. What it obscures: 'largest' depends on measurement method, and the claim is sometimes challenged by the British Empire's total territory. The factoid's popularity reflects how the Mongol Empire has become shorthand for a kind of world-historical scale that defies ordinary comprehension.
